skill.name: seedance-v2
skill.description: Generate short cinematic videos with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro through the local RunComfy CLI.
skill.summary: Create 4-15 second Seedance 2.0 Pro videos with text, image, video, and audio references.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown examples, inline code, placeholder URLs, or line-context false positives. The confirmed risks are legitimate but meaningful: the skill instructs agents to run the local RunComfy CLI, send prompts and media references to RunComfy over HTTPS, download generated files, and rely on local token storage.

medium_findings.title: Local CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill directs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI and invoke the Seedance endpoint through shell commands. This is expected for the skill, but it means an agent may execute local commands and should confirm inputs, output paths, and account context before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and explanation clearly instruct use of the local runcomfy executable. The surrounding text describes a legitimate workflow, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Submission
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill sends prompt content and reference media URLs to RunComfy model services, then downloads generated output URLs. This can expose user prompts, media references, and generated assets to a third-party provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicitly documented, including the API endpoint and generated-output download domains. No hidden callback or unrelated exfiltration endpoint was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Credential File Documented
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents that RunComfy login stores an API token in a hidden configuration file. This is normal CLI behavior, but marketplace users should understand that credentials are stored locally unless RUNCOMFY_TOKEN is used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path is directly documented and includes owner-only permissions. The risk is credential exposure from local filesystem compromise, not malicious token harvesting by the skill text.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The repeated Ruby/shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code spans and fenced command examples. They do not indicate Ruby backtick execution or dynamic shell evaluation inside executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation, and the flagged backticks are visible Markdown formatting or shell examples. I found no Ruby source file or code path that evaluates backtick expressions.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URL Path Traversal Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The path traversal detections occur on placeholder reference URLs such as image, video, and audio examples. These are not filesystem paths and do not contain an executable traversal routine.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequence is part of abbreviated HTTPS examples. No evidence found of local path normalization, file reads, or traversal-sensitive file access at these lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Line-Context False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings map to prose about model description, routing rules, prompting guidance, and exit-code documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic primitives, hashing logic, system inventory, or reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary documentation text, not code. The scanner likely matched substrings without semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples For RunComfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command examples that agents may execute. They are scoped to the documented RunComfy CLI workflow, but users should review prompts and output directories before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is present in fenced Bash examples. It is not malicious by itself, but it is a real execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Model API And Output Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow sends requests to a RunComfy model API and downloads generated output from RunComfy domains. This should be disclosed because prompts and media references leave the local machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and output domains are directly stated in the skill. The behavior is transparent and limited to the model workflow.

content.value_statement: Short video generation often needs careful model routing, reference media limits, and CLI parameters. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Seedance 2.0 Pro prompts, inputs, and RunComfy execution.
actual_capabilities: Routes explicit Seedance and ByteDance video requests to the Seedance 2.0 Pro endpoint.
Documents supported prompt, image, video, audio, duration, aspect ratio, resolution, audio, and seed inputs.
Provides RunComfy CLI examples for text-only, image-reference, and multimodal video generation.
Explains when to choose Seedance 2.0 Pro instead of HappyHorse, Wan, Kling, or LTX models.
Gives prompt guidance for camera motion, audio direction, identity stability, and reference media use.
Summarizes RunComfy exit codes, token handling, output downloads, and privacy boundaries.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Only documents 4-15 second clips and up to 720p output for this endpoint.
Reference videos and audio must meet the model limits described in the skill.
Generated videos depend on upstream model availability, prompt clarity, and RunComfy service behavior.

use_cases.title: Create Lip-Synced Ads
use_cases.description: Generate short spokesperson or product videos with image references, voice direction, and vertical social formats.

use_cases.title: Prototype Cinematic Shots
use_cases.description: Turn shot descriptions, camera movement, and reference media into quick visual previews for planning.

use_cases.title: Build Repeatable Video Tests
use_cases.description: Use fixed schema fields and seeds to compare prompts, references, and aspect ratios across variants.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Text Video
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a short text-only clip.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Seedance 2.0 Pro to create a five second video from this idea: [describe the scene]. Use 720p, generated audio, and a clear camera direction.

prompt_templates.title: Image Reference Spokesperson
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request using one identity reference.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an eight second vertical Seedance video using this image as the character reference: [image URL]. The person says: [script]. Keep the identity stable and use a medium close-up.

prompt_templates.title: Multimodal Scene Match
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with image, video, and audio references.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Seedance clip that combines this subject image, scene video, and voice reference: [URLs]. Match the setting and tone while following this action: [action].

prompt_templates.title: Model Fit Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this video goal and decide whether Seedance 2.0 Pro is the right RunComfy model. Explain the choice, required inputs, risks, and final CLI plan before running anything.

output_examples.input: A request for a 9:16 cafe spokesperson clip using one headshot and a short product line.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Seedance 2.0 Pro, drafts a concise prompt, confirms the image reference, sets an eight second duration, and prepares a RunComfy CLI invocation.

output_examples.input: A request to extend a clip beyond 15 seconds.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the endpoint duration limit, recommends splitting the concept into multiple segments, and outlines prompts for each segment.

output_examples.input: A request with image, video, and audio references for a brand narrative.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks reference count and duration limits, converts the creative brief into model fields, and calls out privacy considerations before submission.

best_practices: Use image references for stable identity, logos, and costumes instead of long verbal descriptions.
Keep action, camera motion, lighting, and audio direction clear and non-conflicting.
Confirm that all media references are approved for third-party processing before running the CLI.
anti_patterns: Do not send private media URLs unless the user accepts RunComfy processing.
Do not ask this endpoint for clips longer than 15 seconds in one run.
Do not mix unrelated visual styles, aspect ratios, or conflicting camera directions in one prompt.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an AI agent to create short Seedance 2.0 Pro videos through the RunComfy CLI.

faq.question: Does it run commands locally?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents RunComfy CLI commands that may be executed on the local machine.

faq.question: Does it send data to a third party?
faq.answer: Yes. Prompts and reference media URLs are sent to RunComfy model services for generation.

faq.question: What media references are supported?
faq.answer: The skill documents image, video, and audio references within the limits supported by the Seedance endpoint.

faq.question: Can it make long videos?
faq.answer: Not in one request. The documented endpoint supports 4-15 second clips.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning because it uses local CLI execution, network submission, and token storage.
